coredata.dc:title: Dual-functionalization of fluorescent carbon dots via cyclodextrin and aminosilane for visual detection of β-glucuronidase and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chenhui
Wu, Meng
Sun, Qijun
Su, Chenglin
Cao, Shuang
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive method for the detection of β-glucuronidase was established using functionalized carbon dots (β-CD-SiCDs) as fluorescent probes. The β-CD-SiCDs were found to be obtained through in situ autopolymerization by mixing the solutions of methyldopa, mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane at room temperature. The method has the characteristics of low energy consumption, simple and rapid. β-CD-SiCDs exhibited green fluorescence at 515 nm emission with a quantum yield of 7.9 %. 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide was introduced as a substrate for β-glucuronidase to generate p-nitrophenol. Subsequently, p-nitrophenol self-assembled with β-CD-SiCDs through host-guest recognition to form a stable inclusion complex, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of β-CD-SiCDs. The linear range of β-CD-SiCDs for detecting β-glucuronidase activity was 0.5–60 U L−1 with a detection limit of 0.14 U L−1. For on-site detection, gel reagents were prepared by a simple method and the images were visualized and quantified by taking advantage of smartphones, avoiding the use of large instrumentation. The constructed fluorescence sensing platform has the benefits of easy operation and time saving, and has been successfully used for the detection of β-glucuronidase activity in serum and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-Glucuronidase
Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Visual detection
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of portable test kits and visual detection of β-Gcu
Choice and optical properties of β-CD-SiCDs
Study on characterization and reaction mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province ( LH2022B002 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2572022DJ01 ), 111 Project ( B20088 ), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team). We would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.

This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022B002), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022DJ01), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team). We would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption spectra of β-CD, DAMO, MDA, β-CD- SiCDs and fluorescence emission spectra of β-CD- SiCDs. The illustration is under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) Fluorescence mapping spectra of β-CD-SiCDs. (C) TEM spectra of β-CD-SiCDs. (D) FRIR spectra of β-CD, DAMO, MDA and β-CD-SiCDs. (E) XPS spectra of β-CD-SiCDs. (F) C 1s, (G) O 1s, (H) N 1s, and (I) Si 2p high-resolution XPS of β-CD-SiCDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of β-CD-SiCDs with PNP. (B) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-SiCDs and the concentration of β-Gcu. (C) Fluorescence lifetime curves of β-CD-SiCDs before and after adding PNP. (D) Comparison of excitation spectra (blue), emission spectra (green) of β-CD-SiCDs and absorption spectra of PNP. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Detection of β-Gcu by host-guest recognition based on β-CD-SiCDs nanoprobes. (B) The feasibility of the sensing strategy. (C) Optimization of incubation time for enzymatic reaction. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of β-CD-SiCDs and β-Gcu at different concentrations. (E) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F–F0)/F0 of β-CD-SiCDs and the concentration of β-Gcu. (F) Fluorescence response of β-CD-SiCDs to the presence of coexisting substances and β-Gcu. (G) Fluorescence response of the system in the presence of β-Gcu with the addition of multiple cofactors.

(A) Procedure for the portable gel reagent. (B) Hydrogel structures containing β-CD-SiCDs. (C) Fluorescence images of β-Gcu at different concentrations reacting with hydrogels containing β-CD-SiCDs under UV lamps at 365 nm, and smartphone-based RGB value recognition.

Cell activity (%) after 24 h culture with different concentrations of (A) β-CD-SiCDs and (B) PNPG. (C) Imaging of endogenous β-Gcu in HeLa cells by β-CD-SiCDs: (a) none, (b)β-CD-SiCDs, (c) β-CD-SiCDs and 0.5 mM PNPG, (d) β-CD-SiCDs and 1 mM PNPG. (D) Imaging of exogenous β-Gcu in HeLa cells by β-CD-SiCDs: (a) none, (b)β-CD-SiCDs, (c) β-CD-SiCDs and 30 U L−1 β-Gcu, (d) β-CD-SiCDs and 60 U L−1 β-Gcu.
Synthesis strategy of β-CD- SiCDs and detection of β-Gcu.
Comparison of β-Gcu detection performance by different methods.

head.ce:title: Dual-functionalization of fluorescent carbon dots via cyclodextrin and aminosilane for visual detection of β-glucuronidase and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin, 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive method for the detection of β-glucuronidase was established using functionalized carbon dots (β-CD-SiCDs) as fluorescent probes. The β-CD-SiCDs were found to be obtained through in situ autopolymerization by mixing the solutions of methyldopa, mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane at room temperature. The method has the characteristics of low energy consumption, simple and rapid. β-CD-SiCDs exhibited green fluorescence at 515 nm emission with a quantum yield of 7.9 %. 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide was introduced as a substrate for β-glucuronidase to generate p-nitrophenol. Subsequently, p-nitrophenol self-assembled with β-CD-SiCDs through host-guest recognition to form a stable inclusion complex, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of β-CD-SiCDs. The linear range of β-CD-SiCDs for detecting β-glucuronidase activity was 0.5–60 U L−1 with a detection limit of 0.14 U L−1. For on-site detection, gel reagents were prepared by a simple method and the images were visualized and quantified by taking advantage of smartphones, avoiding the use of large instrumentation. The constructed fluorescence sensing platform has the benefits of easy operation and time saving, and has been successfully used for the detection of β-glucuronidase activity in serum and cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, as a malignant tumor, has become a serious threat to human life. Tumor marker detection is of great significance for cancer diagnosis and early treatment, leading to a higher chance of survival for patients. As a cancer marker, β-glucuronidase (β-Gcu) catalyzes the degradation of glycosaminoglycans on extracellular matrix and cell membranes in normal and cancer tissues [1]. Increased β-Gcu activity has been reported to induce several cancers, such as liver cancer [2], colon cancer [3], kidney cancer [4], and bladder cancer [5]. Serum β-Gcu overexpression is also strongly associated with a number of pathological conditions, including kidney disease, larynx, and breast [6]. As a result, it is crucial to establish β-Gcu-specific and sensitive assays.

To date, several β-Gcu detection methods have been developed, including electrochemical [7], colorimetric [8], and fluorescence [9] methods. Among them, fluorescence methods have the advantages of high sensitivity, real-time detection, and especially non-invasive detection capability are of great interest in biosensing and imaging. So far, several fluorescence methods have been developed to detect β-Gcu. However, most of the fluorescent probes or substrates are complicated to synthesize and have poor water solubility, which is not conducive to practical applications. To solve the problem of water solubility, dimethyl sulfoxide is usually used to dissolve fluorescent probes. However, the introduction of organic solvents into the detection system affects the enzyme activity and reduces the sensitivity and accuracy of the method. Therefore, there is an urgent need to establish novel fluorophore-based methods for β-Gcu detection.

Carbon dots, as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with tunable optical properties, good water solubility, and low biotoxicity, have attracted a lot of attention in biomedical and analytical sensing [10,11]. There are various precursors for the preparation of carbon dots. Organosilane-functionalized carbon dots are particularly suitable for sensing in aqueous samples because of their good biocompatibility, aqueous dispersibility, cost-effectiveness, and surface functionalization capabilities. A variety of organosilane-functionalized carbon dots have been successfully synthesized by hydrothermal and microwave [12,13]. However, these methods require significant energy consumption, including microwave radiation, high temperature and high pressure [14]. Therefore, there is a need in developing low energy consumption and environmentally friendly synthesis methods. In recent years, the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots by self-polymerization from dopamine has attracted much attention. Buffer solutions are usually used to provide alkaline conditions, but the prepared carbon dots have weak fluorescence [15]. Liu prepared carbon dots from polyethyleneimine and dopamine at room temperature [16]. However, the surface groups of carbon dots prepared by this method were relatively homogeneous. Exploring the type of reactants is also important for the room temperature synthesis of carbon dots. Certain aqueous solutions of organosilanes provide alkaline conditions and have the potential to prepare organosilane-functionalized carbon dots with low energy consumption and in an environmentally friendly manner. In addition, hydrogels are widely used in encapsulation and fixation, and are expected to be used as solid phase carriers for on-site detection.

In recent years, the research of biosensors using fluorescence on/off systems has become increasingly active. For example, a biosensor using β-cyclodextrin (a cyclic receptor linked by seven glucose units) could detect guest molecular complexes in a β-cyclodextrin cavity by introducing fluorescent compounds [17]. β-Cyclodextrin-modified fluorescent materials have been used as fluorescent sensors in various quenching methods, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer, contact quenching, and photoinduced electron transfer [18,19].

In this study, functionalized carbon dots were prepared by Michael addition and Schiff base reaction of methyldopa sesquihydrate, N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO) and cyclodextrin as precursors in room temperature The prepared β-CD-SiCDs exhibited excellent optical properties and could be used as fluorescent probes. Through host-guest recognition and photoinduced electron transfer, p-nitrophenol (PNP) can significantly reduce the fluorescence of β-CD-SiCDs. β-Gcu can specifically hydrolyze the substrate 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) to release PNP, which in turn quenches the fluorescence of β-CD-SiCDs. Further, β-Gcu gel reagents were prepared for rapid detection of β-Gcu by introducing β-CD-SiCDs into hydrogels. The combination of smartphone image collection and quantification made the detection results more accurate compared to naked eye observation. The method achieved satisfactory results in serum samples. The biological indicators of β-CD-SiCDs in cell systems were also investigated, and β-Gcu sensing and imaging in cells were successfully achieved (Scheme 1 ).

In a typical preparation, 200 μL of DAMO, 23.8 mg of methyldopa sesquihydrate and 20 mg of mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin were added to 5 mL of deionized water. The mixed solution was kept for 1.5 h without heating, during which the color of the solution changed from colorless to brownish yellow. Subsequently, an excess of sodium borohydride was added and stirred uniformly for 30 min to turn light yellow. The large particles were removed by filtration and further purified with a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 24 h. The powder of β-CD-SiCDs was obtained by freeze-drying under vacuum for 30 h. Finally, the samples were collected for further characterization.

The detection of β-Gcu activity was carried out according to the following steps. The different concentrations of β-Gcu solution (200 μL) and PNPG (100 μL, 10 mM) were diluted to 500 μL with NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 5.0, 20 mM). The reaction system was incubated at a constant temperature of 37 °C for 25 min. After completion of incubation, β-CD-SiCDs (100 μL, 200 μg mL−1) were added and incubated with PBS (pH = 7.4, 50 mM). After incubation for 2 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable test kits and visual detection of β-Gcu
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized β-CD-SiCDs were firstly rooted in sodium alginate, which was dropped on the snap cap of a centrifuge tube to form a hydrogel by introducing Ca2+ in a single step. Subsequently, different concentrations of β-Gcu solution (200 μL) and PNPG (100 μL, 10 mM) were diluted to 500 μL with NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 5.0, 20 mM), and the reaction system was placed at a constant temperature of 37 °C for 25 min. After completing the incubation, the solutions were transferred to microtubes and incubated with PBS (pH = 7.4, 50 mM). After fastening the cap, the microtubes were inverted for 20 min to allow the solution to penetrate into the β-CD-SiCDs-based hydrogel. β-CD-SiCDs undergo supramolecular self-assembly with PNP due to host-guest recognition. Pictures of the color change of the hydrogels caused by different concentrations of β-Gcu were collected by a smartphone camera. Fluorescence images were converted to specific color intensities via a color scanning app (from WeChat app) for smartphone.
Detailed information on human serum sample processing and cell culture and sensing experiments is described in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice and optical properties of β-CD-SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that dopamine and silane can easily produce fluorescent carbon polymer dots at room temperature, and it is presumed that dopamine analogues can also undergo fluorescence reactions [20]. Therefore, fluorescent CDs were synthesized at room temperature by the one-pot method by choosing methyldopa as the carbon source and DAMO and β-cyclodextrin as the reactants. β-CD-SiCDs optical properties were investigated. β-CD-SiCDs showed new absorption peaks at 284 (π-π*) and 337 nm (n-π*), which were different from the absorption spectra of raw materials such as dopamine, DAMO, and β-CD [21,22]. β-CD-SiCDs aqueous solutions are orange-yellow in daylight and show bright green light under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1 A). It is noteworthy that the aqueous solutions of the starting materials do not exhibit fluorescent properties. These phenomena indicate that β-CD-SiCDs have been successfully prepared. The fluorescence mapping spectra indicated the optimal excitation (380 nm) and emission (515 nm), respectively (Fig. 1B). In addition, the emission spectra were scanned for an increase in excitation wavelength from 330 to 430 nm. As a result, the β-CD-SiCDs exhibit non-excitation dependence at excitations of 330–430 nm in Fig. S6.

The influence of pH value on fluorescence intensity was further studied. β-CD-SiCDs showed good fluorescence performance in milder environments (pH 5–10), and the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-SiCDs was maintained above 50 % even under strong acid or strong alkaline conditions (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S2A, β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged within 90 min of UV irradiation, indicating high resistance to photobleaching. The fluorescence intensity remains stable even in solutions with NaCl concentrations as high as 200 mM, indicating the high salt tolerance (Fig. S2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on characterization and reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of β-CD-SiCDs were investigated. The morphology of β-CD-SiCDs was revealed by TEM (Fig. 1C). β-CD-SiCDs showed a sphere-like shape with a size distribution ranging from 2.4 nm to 6.5 nm and an average diameter of 3.9 nm. Furthermore, according to HRTEM, a clear lattice stripe was observed for β-CD-SiCDs. The lattice fringe is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to a graphite (100) structure [41]. These results indicate that β-CD-SiCDs have good stability and dispersion in water. Compared to the FT-IR spectra of methyldopa, cyclodextrin, and DAMO, a new signal appeared for β-CD-SiCDs at 1694 cm−1. It could be attributed to CONH unit, indicating the occurrence of Schiff base with Michael addition [23] (Fig. 1D). The signal at 1402 cm−1 was attributed to the C–N stretching mode and 3421 cm−1 to the hydroxyl or amino stretching vibration. β-CD-SiCDs are rich in hydrophilic groups on the surface making good water dispersion [24].

XPS experiments were used to study the elemental composition and chemical bonding of β-CD-SiCDs. The β-CD-SiCDs contain C, O, N, and Si elements with 57.69 %, 23.32 %, 9.78 %, and 9.21 % of each element, respectively (Fig. 1E). The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum can be resolved into four types. The four binding energies are 283.8 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.8 eV, and 287.7 eV (Fig. 1F), which are attributed to Si–C, C–C/CC, C–N, and C–O, respectively [25]. The O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1G) shows three distinct peaks at 530.7 eV, 531.5 eV, and 533.2 eV corresponding to the Si–O, C–O, and CO groups, respectively [26]. The N 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum near 399.4 eV can be decomposed into three peaks (Fig. 1H) corresponding to three N states, namely N–C/N–Si (398.4 eV), C–N–C (399.7 eV), and N–H [27] (400.9 eV). The Si 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum near 101.6 eV can be decomposed into three peaks [28] (Fig. 1I) corresponding to three Si states, namely Si–C (101.0 eV), Si–N (101.7 eV) and Si–O (102.4 eV). The results of XPS analysis were compatible with those of FT-IR analysis. In conclusion, β-CD-SiCDs containing abundant functional groups were successfully synthesized.

Dopamine could react with Schiff bases by Michael addition with amino-containing substances under basic and aerobic conditions. It is presumed that the mechanism of synthesis of β-CD-SiCDs is similar to the above. A sufficient amount of NaBH4 was added to methyl dopamine followed by DAMO and β-CD. No fluorescence was observed, probably because NaBH4 removed the possible production of quinone derivatives thus preventing the production of fluorescent β-CD-SiCDs [29]. The absence of fluorescent signal production after replacing DAMO and β-cyclodextrin with sodium hydroxide suggests that the amino group is essential in the generation of β-CD-SiCDs [30]. In addition, NaBH4 was added to the prepared β-CD-SiCDs solution in order to inhibit the uncontrolled autoxidation process. Notably, the fluorescence of the solution was significantly enhanced by the addition of NaBH4. The quantum yield of NaBH4-treated β-CD-SiCDs was 7.9 %, which was higher than that without NaBH4 treatment (2.6 %).

To further investigate the reaction mechanism, QTOF-MS mass spectrometry characterization (positive mode) was performed. The three major fragmentation peaks at m/z at 327, 462 and 655 may be attributed to the possible chemical structures of a, b and c, respectively (Fig. S3). These structures confirm that the reaction of methyldopa with DAMO and cyclodextrin includes Michael addition, self-polymerization reactions with Schiff base reactions [31]. This is consistent with the literature reporting that dopamine is prone to oxidation and thus Michael addition, Schiff base reaction and autopolymerization with ethylenediamine analogues under alkaline conditions [32]. In conclusion, the nonfluorescent precursors underwent a series of reactions to finally generate fluorescent β-CD-SiCDs.

Due to the large binding constant (2.3 × 103 M−1) [33], β-CD can contain PNP in its hydrophobic cavity to form supramolecular host-guest complexes [34]. This process shortens the PNP-fluorescent nanomaterial distance and generates strong interactions. The effect of PNP on β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence was investigated. With the increase of PNP, β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence gradually quenched (Fig. 2 A). Notably, 100 μM of PNP could quench β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence by about 76 % and the CDs and PNP concentration showed a good linear relationship in the range of 1–100 μM (F = -20.6CPNP+2624.2). The detection limit is 0.27 μM by calculating the ratio of the standard deviation of blank measurement to the slope of linear equation. This indicates that the probe is sensitive to PNP, which provides the possibility for trace detection of β-Gcu (Fig. 2B). As a good electron acceptor, PNP can quench the fluorescent probe by photoinduced electron transfer. To better investigate whether the quenching mechanism is photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the fluorescence lifetime of β-CD-SiCDs before and after quenching was first examined. The fluorescence lifetime decreased from 5.09 ns to 4.55 ns after addition, which is consistent with PET and excludes static quenching (Fig. 2C). The PNP absorption spectra were also examined with the β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence spectra. There was no huge overlap between them, which ruled out the inner filter effect and Förster resonance energy transfer (Fig. 2D). In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of PNP on β-CD-SiCDs was attributed to PET. In addition, the effect of PNP on cyclodextrin-free modified SiCDs fluorescence was also investigated. The fluorescence quenching of cyclodextrin-free modified SiCDs was only 37 % after the addition of 100 μM PNP concentration, which was about twice less than that of cyclodextrin-modified SiCDs. This indicates that the supramolecular self-assembly process plays an important role in the fluorescence quenching.

The sensitive fluorescence response of β-CD-SiCDs to PNP offers the possibility of β-Gcu detection (Fig. 3 A). β-Gcu specifically hydrolyzes the substrate PNPG to release PNP, which in turn quenching β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence (Fig. 3B). The effect of β-Gcu and PNPG on β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence was first examined. β-Gcu and PNPG alone were negligible for β-CD-SiCDs, and only when β-Gcu and PNPG were present together, β-CD-SiCD fluorescence was significantly quenched. This suggests that PNP generated by the enzymatic reaction enters the β-CD hydrophobic cavity to quench the β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence, verifying the feasibility of this strategy.

To obtain better results, the experimental conditions were optimized including pH values of the reaction system and the reaction time. The changes in the reaction dynamics of the system were recorded by measuring the fluorescence intensity every 5 min. The fluorescence intensity was continuously enhanced for 25 min and then remained stable (Fig. 3C). The enzymatic reaction was carried out in NaAc-HAc buffer at different pH values, and the results showed that β-Gcu had the strongest activity at pH = 5.0 (Fig. S4). Therefore, the optimal pH value was 5.0 and the reaction time was 25 min.

Under optimal conditions, β-CD-SiCDs were investigated for β-Gcu activity sensing. With the increase of β-Gcu activity, β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence was gradually quenched (Fig. 3D). (F0–F)/F0 showed a good linear relationship with β-Gcu activity in the range of 0.5–60 U L−1 (Fig. 3E), with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.14 U L−1. The regression direction was described as y = 0.0061x+0.028, where the correlation coefficient was 0.995.

A number of coexisting substances that may have come from biological samples were examined in selective experiments. Most coexisting substances such as metal ions, amino acids, glucose, ascorbic acid, glutathione, and various other biological enzymes did not significantly affect fluorescence (Fig. 3F). β-Gcu was the only substance that significantly changed β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence. In Fig. 3G, the addition of interfering substances did not cause changes in β-Gcu fluorescence. The results indicate that these interfering substances have negligible effects on β-Gcu. The established fluorescence sensing method showed good selectivity for β-Gcu, mainly due to the unique ability of β-Gcu to directionally hydrolyze PNPG.

Hydrogels with three-dimensional mesh structure have a wide prospect in visualizing sensing systems due to their strong loading power and low background color [35]. Sodium alginate is a natural polysaccharide with strong biocompatibility and stability. In the presence of calcium ions, its linear structure undergoes stacking to form a three-dimensional meshwork, thus forming a hydrogel in a simple manner with a strong ability to absorb and release solutions. In the presence of calcium ions, the linear structures are stacked to form a three-dimensional network structure, thus forming a hydrogel with a strong ability to absorb and release solutions in a simple manner [36]. In this study, a portable gel reagent for visualization of sensing β-Gcu using β-CD-SiCDs as probes was constructed. The steps of the portable gel reagent are shown in Fig. 4 . First, the hydrogel rooted with β-CD-SiCDs was injected into the cap of the test tube. After the β-Gcu-containing solution was added to the test tube, repeated rotations were performed to diffuse the p-nitrophenol produced by enzymatic hydrolysis of PNPG into the β-CD-SiCDs-SA hydrogel (Fig. 4A). To facilitate the acquisition of hydrogel color information, the test tube was restored to its original state. The color of hydrogel was captured by smartphone and converted into a digital signal in combination with an applet to quantitatively assess β-Gcu. In this strategy, p-nitrophenol was still able to quench β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence implanted in the hydrogel by host-guest recognition (Fig. 4B). To verify the feasibility of this strategy, the hydrogel color under the influence of different concentrations of β-Gcu was examined. The hydrogel color gradually faded from green when the β-Gcu concentration increased under UV light. The smartphone collected the hydrogel color by software conversion to RGB signal, and the green channel responded to the obvious concentration gradient change (Fig. 4C). The hydrogel conversion intensity (G/G0) showed linear relationship with β-Gcu in the range of 1–60 U L−1 (Fig. S5). The regression direction is described as y = -0.0076x+0.97, where the correlation coefficient is 0.992.

The β-Gcu fluorescence sensing system was investigated for its sensing ability in real samples. The analysis results of three human serum samples were presented in Table S1. The values of unspiked samples were determined by spectrometer to be 7.3, 8.7, 6.4, 6.8, and 8.1 U L−1, respectively. The accuracy of the proposed method was evaluated by spiking a certain amount of β-Gcu solution to the above samples. The average recoveries of β-Gcu were 96.3%–102.9 % with RSD less than 3.8 % based on spectrometer measurements. In addition, the results of the above three unspiked samples were 7.2, 8.3, 6.6, 6.9, and 7.7 U L−1 based on smartphone integrated gel reagents, and the average recoveries obtained from the spiking experiments were 98.2%–104.1 % with RSD less than 4.3 % (Table S2). It showed that the β-Gcu fluorescence sensing strategy has good accuracy. Unspiked samples were also analyzed by the traditional colorimetric method [37]. The results were 7.5, 8.6, 6.2, 7.1, and 7.8 U L−1, which demonstrated that the established method detection results were consistent with those of the colorimetric method. However, the conventional colorimetric method relies on a chromogenic substrate (p-nitrophenol) with a low molar absorption coefficient, resulting in insufficient sensitivity. The fluorescence method proposed in this paper can make the results more sensitive by modifying the carbon dots with functional groups or increasing the fluorescence quantum yield of the fluorescent probe. The results show that other biomolecules in serum samples are negligible for β-Gcu detection, and the method is applicable to β-Gcu detection in real samples. A comparison with other methods for the detection of β-Gcu was also listed (Table 1 ). The LOD of this method is lower than most colorimetric and fluorescent methods and has a wider linear range, which provides a basis for further detection of other biological samples. Fluorescent probes can be widely used due to the significant advantages of rapidity, simplicity and low cost.

The β-Gcu imaging study in HeLa cells was carried out to broaden the applicability of β-CD-SiCDs. The cytotoxicity of β-CD-SiCDs and PNPG was evaluated via MTT assay. Fig. 5 A shows HeLa cell viability after 24 h of co-culture with β-CD-SiCDs (25–400 μg mL−1). Even with β-CD-SiCDs concentrations up to 400 μg mL−1, HeLa cell activity was higher than 90 %, indicating a satisfactory low cytotoxicity of β-CD-SiCDs. Cytotoxicity was also investigated in the presence of PNPG. Fig. 5B shows high cell viability even up to 1 mM PNPG. Inspired by the excellent low cytotoxicity and selectivity, intracellular β-Gcu imaging studies were carried out (Fig. 5C). To confirm that the collected green fluorescence was non-background fluorescence, cells without β-CD-SiCDs treatment were used as a control group. It can be seen that cells untreated with β-CD-SiCDs have no fluorescence signal at the green channel. While cells treated with β-CD-SiCDs exhibited strong fluorescence signal at the green channel. Cells that had ingested β-CD-SiCDs were also incubated with different concentrations of PNPG. As the concentration of PNPG increased, the cells showed gradually weaker fluorescence signal at the green channel. This may be due to the hydrolysis of intracellular PNPG by β-Gcu to produce PNP, which in turn enters the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD to quench the fluorescence of β-CD-SiCDs. The higher the concentration of substrate PNPG, the more PNP is produced, and therefore the more obvious the fluorescence quenching.

In addition, exogenous β-Gcu fluorescence imaging studies were carried out (Fig. 5D). Cells untreated with β-CD-SiCDs were performed as controls and showed no fluorescence signal at the green channel. In contrast, cells treated with β-CD-SiCDs containing PNPG exhibited a strong fluorescence signal at the green channel. The cells were also incubated with different concentrations of β-Gcu and cells that had ingested β-CD-SiCDs. As the concentration of β-Gcu increased, the cells showed progressively weaker fluorescence signal at the green channel. A possible explanation is that β-Gcu hydrolyzed intracellular PNPG to produce PNP, which in turn entered the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD to quench the fluorescence of β-CD-SiCDs. The stronger the activity of β-Gcu, the more PNP was produced, and thus the more pronounced the fluorescence quenching was. It can be concluded that the proposed method is appropriate for β-Gcu imaging and biosensing in living cells.

In this work, β-CD-SiCDs were prepared by in situ synthesis using methyldopa, DAMO and cyclodextrin as raw materials and sodium borohydride as stabilizer. β-CD-SiCDs exhibited bright green fluorescence at 515 nm under 380 nm excitation with a QY of 7.9 %. PNP can significantly reduce β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence through supramolecular self-assembly. β-Gcu can specifically hydrolyze the substrate PNPG to release PNP, which in turn quenching β-CD-SiCDs fluorescence. Further β-CD-SiCDs were introduced into the hydrogel to prepare a rapid detection gel reagent. In addition, the introduction of smartphones made this fluorescence visual sensing more accurate than naked eye observation. The present work is of great significance in nanomaterial preparation and biosensing applications. The Michael addition and Schiff base reaction-driven self-polymerization strategy was developed for the preparation of β-CD-SiCDs, which opens up a new avenue for low-energy, simple and rapid preparation of fluorescent CDs. The encapsulation of β-CD-SiCDs with hydrogels enhanced the portability of the assay reagents and combined with smartphones for quantitative analysis, which is useful for immediate on-site detection. β-Gcu was sensed and imaged using human serum samples and HeLa cells as models and satisfactory results were obtained. This work contributes to our further understanding of the role of β-Gcu in biological systems, especially in diseases.

Chenhui Yin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Meng Wu: Data curation, Investigation. Qijun Sun: Methodology. Chenglin Su: Investigation. Shuang Cao: Conceptualization. Na Niu: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ligang Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022B002), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022DJ01), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team). We would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenhui Yin is a Doctor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is biological analysis.

Meng Wu is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is biology and her research direction is chemical biology.

Qijun Sun is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is chemical biology.

Chenglin Su is a Master student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their visualization applications.

Shuang Cao is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is biology and her research direction is chemical biology.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are fluorescence probes and molecular imprinting techniques.
